title: Kinetic Energy
summary: The energy of motion. See why speed matters far more than mass with ½mv².

steps.title: What is kinetic energy?
steps.body:
Anything that moves carries energy — the **energy of motion**. We call it **kinetic energy** (KE).

A tossed tennis ball barely nudges a box. A bowling ball at the same speed flattens it. Speed that ball up and it hits even harder. So two things set KE: how **heavy** something is, and how **fast** it moves.

steps.title: The formula
steps.body:
Here's how we measure it. **m** is mass (in kg), **v** is speed (in m/s).

Notice **v is squared** — speed counts far more than mass. Double the speed and KE jumps by four.

steps.title: Get a feel for it
steps.body: Don't just take the formula's word for it — play. Drag mass and speed and watch KE react. Then try to hit the goal.

steps.prompt: A car speeds up from 20 mph to 40 mph — it doubles its speed. What happens to its kinetic energy?
hints: Good try — but notice KE depends on v², not just v.
You're close. When the speed doubles, the squared term grows by 2 × 2 — think about what that does to KE.
steps.solution:
Kinetic energy depends on the square of speed: KE = ½mv². When the speed doubles, the v² term grows by 2² = 4, while the mass stays the same.

**So the answer is "It quadruples (×4)".**

steps.prompt: Let's plug in real numbers. A block has **m = 3 kg** and **v = 2 m/s**. Drag each value into the right spot in the equation.
steps.fillHint: Almost — mass goes in the m spot and speed in the v spot. Here m = 3 and v = 2.
computeHints: So close — square the speed first (v × v), then bring in the mass and the ½.
Step by step: square v, multiply by m, then take half of the result.
steps.solution:
Kinetic energy is KE = ½mv².

Square the speed first: v² = 2² = 4. Then bring in the mass and the half: KE = ½ × 3 × 4 = **6 J**.

steps.title: Work changes KE
steps.body: Remember the work-energy theorem from Lesson 1? The **net work** done on an object equals its **change in kinetic energy**.

steps.prompt: **Net work = 24 J** is done on a **2 kg** block starting from rest. Drag **ΔKE**.
hints: Not quite — remember the work-energy theorem: W_net = ΔKE.
All of the net work goes straight into motion, so ΔKE matches the W_net you're given.
steps.solution:
By the work-energy theorem, the net work done on an object equals its change in kinetic energy: W_net = ΔKE.

Here W_net = 24 J, so ΔKE = **24 J**.

steps.title: Lesson complete!
steps.body: You learned what kinetic energy is, why speed matters most (v²), and how work changes it.
